docs.title: 260626   BOOK
docs.text:
Digital Imaging – Technical Foundations

An Online Book about Digital images – Technical Foundations

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this series on the technical side of digital animation.

There is no shortage of tutorials online that show you how to make animations. Animators explain software, workflows, and tricks. You can find countless videos demonstrating tips, techniques, and shortcuts.

However, if you want to understand what actually happens under the hood—what occurs when you apply a filter, choose a colour, or render an image—you’ll quickly find that this type of knowledge is much harder to access. The information exists, but it is scattered across technical articles and documentation. Finding it often feels like searching for a needle in a haystack.

This book series aims to bring that knowledge together.

Rather than focusing on specific software, we will explore the underlying principles of digital animation. The goal is to provide a fundamental understanding that applies across all tools and platforms. Whether you use Blender, Maya, After Effects, or any other software, the concepts remain the same.

These materials are originally developed for my students at the art academy St. Joost in the south of the Netherlands, but they are intended for anyone interested in understanding animation beyond the interface.

Throughout this series, examples will often be demonstrated using open-source software, making the material accessible to everyone.

docs.title: Types of Digital Animation
docs.text: The term digital animation covers a wide range of techniques. Each type has its own workflow, tools, and required skill set. To understand the technical side of animation, it is important to first distinguish between the main forms.

docs.title: 1.1 Hand-Drawn Animation (2D Animation)
docs.text:
Hand-drawn animation—also known as traditional or cel animation—is one of the oldest forms of animation.

At its core, this technique involves drawing each frame individually. Historically, these drawings were created on paper or transparent acetate sheets (called cels), which is where the term cel animation originates.

While the tools have evolved, the principle remains the same.

  • In the early days, animators worked entirely by hand using paper and paint.
  • In the 1990s, drawings were often created on paper but then scanned and coloured digitally.
  • Today, most hand-drawn animation is produced directly on computers using drawing tablets.

Despite this technological shift, the defining feature remains: every frame is crafted manually, following the same principles developed during the early decades of animation.

Hand-drawn animation can therefore exist in three forms:

  • fully analog
  • fully digital
  • or a hybrid of both

docs.title: 1.2 Stop Motion Animation
docs.text:
Stop motion animation works fundamentally differently. Instead of drawing frames, it involves manipulating real-world objects.

The process is simple in principle:

  1. Move an object slightly
  2. Take a photograph
  3. Repeat

When these images are played in sequence, they create the illusion of motion.

Stop motion is incredibly versatile. It can be created using:

  • everyday objects
  • puppets
  • clay
  • large-scale constructions
  • or even microscopic materials

Common Forms of Stop Motion

Puppet Animation Most professional stop-motion films use puppets built around an internal structure called an armature. This acts as a skeleton, allowing the figure to hold poses and remain stable during animation.

Claymation In this form, characters and environments are made from malleable materials such as plasticine. Because the material can be reshaped, it allows for expressive and organic motion.

Cut-Out Animation (2D Stop Motion) This technique uses flat, pre-cut shapes that are moved frame by frame and typically filmed from above. It bridges the gap between 2D animation and physical stop motion.

docs.title: 1.3 Computer-Generated Animation (3D / CG Animation)
docs.text:
Computer-generated animation (CG or CGI) is the most recent major development in animation.

Unlike hand-drawn animation, CG animation is not created by drawing each frame. Instead, it involves building digital objects and controlling them within a virtual environment.

A useful way to think about 3D animation is:

It is closer to puppetry than drawing.

How It Works

  1. Modeling – Characters and objects are created digitally
  2. Rigging – A virtual skeleton is added to control movement
  3. Animation – The animator sets key poses (keyframes)
  4. Interpolation – The computer calculates the motion between these poses

This process makes CG animation highly controllable and flexible.

Relation to Other Techniques

  • Like stop motion, it involves posing objects in space
  • Like hand-drawn animation, it still relies on frame sequences
  • But it differs in that the computer automates parts of the process

docs.title: 1.4 What All Animation Has in Common
docs.text:
Despite their differences, all animation techniques share a fundamental characteristic:

They are ultimately displayed as a sequence of images on a screen.

Today, these images are almost always digital. That means they are made up of pixels.

Understanding pixels—and how digital images work—is essential for understanding modern animation. This will be explored in detail in a later chapter.

Coming Up Next

Before diving into digital image theory, we will first take a closer look at the tools used in animation:

docs.title: Software and Workflow in Digital Animation
2.1 Introduction
docs.text:
Creating animation requires more than just animation software.

Before anything starts moving, ideas must be developed, written, and visualized. A script needs to be written, a storyboard must be created, and designs have to be developed. At the same time, the entire process must be carefully planned and organized.

This complete system—where all parts of production work together—is called the pipeline.

In general, animation production is divided into three main phases:

  • Preproduction – concept, writing, and design
  • Production – creating the animation
  • Postproduction – editing, compositing, and finishing

In this chapter, we will look at the software used throughout these phases, with a focus on accessible and open-source tools.

docs.title: 2.2 Open-Source Software
docs.text:
Open-source software is released under a license that makes its source code publicly available.

In practice, this means:

  • it is free to use
  • it is often developed collaboratively
  • and it can compete with professional commercial software

For students and independent creators, open-source tools provide a strong and flexible starting point.

docs.title: 2.3 Software Categories in the Animation Pipeline
docs.text:
Before looking at individual programs, it is useful to understand that animation software generally falls into a few key categories:

  • Writing and planning tools
  • Design and image creation tools
  • Animation tools
  • Compositing and editing tools
  • Sound tools

Understanding these categories is more important than any specific program. Software changes over time, but the pipeline remains largely the same.

docs.title: Scriptwriting
docs.text:
Professional studios often use tools such as:

  • Final Draft
  • Celtx
  • StudioBinder

However, these can be expensive. Good free alternatives include:

  • Trelby
  • Fountain
  • Scrite

Storytelling itself falls outside the scope of this series, but there are many excellent resources available. A recommended starting point is Animated Storytelling by Liz Blazer.

docs.title: Drawing and Sketching
docs.text:
Early ideas are often developed through sketches. This can be done on paper, which is still a very strong starting point.

For digital work, the following tools are excellent:

  • Krita – powerful and widely used for digital painting and drawing
  • GIMP – best suited for image manipulation and photo editing
  • Affinity designer (not open source but still free)

Commercial tools like Adobe Photoshop are widely used in the industry, but open-source alternatives are more than capable. If you want to work in the industry it can be wise to invest in adobe products.

docs.title: Graphic Design (Vector Graphics)
docs.text:
For clean lines and scalable graphics, vector-based tools are used.

  • Inkscape – the main open-source alternative to Adobe Illustrator

This is especially useful for:

  • logos
  • stylized artwork
  • technical or graphic animation styles

Commercial tools like Adobe Illustrator are widely used in the industry.

docs.title: 3D Sculpting (Design Phase)
docs.text:
For artists who prefer shaping rather than drawing, digital sculpting offers a more tactile workflow.

  • Industry standard: ZBrush
  • Best open-source solution: Blender (sculpt mode)

Smaller tools like SculptGL exist, but Blender has largely replaced them in most workflows.

docs.title: Storyboarding and Animatics
docs.text:
Once writing and design are in place, the next step is to assemble the story visually.

Storyboarding software helps with:

  • organizing panels
  • adjusting timing
  • rearranging shots

Recommended tools:

  • Storyboarder (open-source)
  • Toon Boom Storyboard Pro (industry standard)

These tools can export sequences to create an animatic, a rough version of the final film that includes timing and sometimes basic sound.

docs.title: Sound (Pre- and postproduction Stage)
docs.text:
Sound work often begins during the animatic phase.

Typical tasks:

  • recording dialogue
  • placing temporary sound effects
  • rough sound editing
  • Audacity is the most widely used open-source audio editor for this purpose

docs.title: Hand-Drawn (2D) Animation
docs.text:
Beginner-friendly tools:

  • Pencil2D
  • Krita

More advanced options:

  • Tahoma2D / OpenToonz – professional-level, open-source
  • Blender (Grease Pencil) – combines 2D and 3D workflows

Professional tools include TV-Paint, Moho and Toon Boom Harmony.

docs.title: Stop Motion Animation
docs.text:
Stop-motion animation requires software to capture and organize frames.

  • Industry standard: Dragonframe

Open/free alternatives:

  • qStopMotion – the most complete open-source option
  • Stop Motion Studio (not open-source but widely used in education)

docs.title: Computer-Generated (3D / CG) Animation
docs.text:
3D animation is technically the most complex form.

Industry tools:

  • Autodesk Maya
  • Houdini
  • Cinema 4D

Blender

The most important open-source alternative is Blender, which has grown into a complete production suite.

Blender can be used for:

  • 3D modelling
  • animation
  • sculpting
  • compositing
  • video editing
  • even 2D animation

Because of this versatility, it is often described as a “Swiss army knife” of animation software.

For beginners and professionals alike, Blender is a tool you cannot ignore.

docs.title: Postproduction Software
docs.text: Postproduction is often overlooked by beginners, but it is a crucial part of the pipeline.

docs.title: Compositing
docs.text:
Compositing is the process of combining multiple image elements into a final shot.

For example:

  • combining rendered layers
  • adding effects
  • adjusting colour and lighting

Tools:

  • Blender (Compositor)
  • Natron – open-source node-based compositing (similar to Nuke)

Not open source but still free:

  • Da vinci resolve (the Fusion workspace)

docs.title: Video Editing
docs.text:
After animation and compositing, everything needs to be assembled into a final film.

Open-source editing tools:

  • Kdenlive – powerful and widely used
  • Shotcut – simpler and beginner-friendly
  • Blender (basic editing also possible)

Not open Source, but still free

  • Da vinci Resolve

Sound

Sound always starts in preproduction but is refined and finalized during postproduction.

  • dialogue is cleaned
  • sound effects are layered
  • music is added
  • Audacity can also be used in this stage

docs.title: Example Workflow (Personal Toolkit)
docs.text:
To illustrate how these tools can work together, here is an example setup:

  • Trelby – scriptwriting
  • Krita – 2D design
  • Inkscape – vector graphics
  • Storyboarder – storyboards and animatics
  • Audacity – sound editing
  • OpenToonz – 2D animation
  • qStopMotion – stop-motion animation
  • Blender – 3D animation, sculpting, compositing, and editing

For most tasks, Blender plays a central role, especially in modern digital workflows.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
If you are new to digital animation, the best approach is to keep things simple:

Start with Krita for drawing, and Blender for animation.

From there, you can gradually expand your toolkit as your projects become more complex.

Coming Up Next: In the next chapter, we move away from software and focus on the foundation of digital imagery:

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Throughout this book, we will work primarily with digital images. Before diving into technical details like pixels and colour, it is important to understand what a digital image actually is. A simple definition might be: A digital image is a visual representation built from data.To compare, Wikipedia defines an image as: “An artifact that depicts visual perception… usually resembling a physical object.”

In other words:

  • an image represents something
  • and uses visual elements—such as colour and shape—to do so

For computers, however, an image is not something visual. It is data that describes visual information. There are two main ways to represent this data. The first is the bitmap image, which is the most important to understand. No matter how an image is created, it will always be displayed on your screen as a bitmap in some form.

docs.title: Three Ingredients of a Digital Image
docs.text:
In simple terms, a computer needs three main components to create an bitmap-image:

  1. Pixels – the smallest building blocks
  2. A structure – a way to organize those pixels
  3. Colour (or grayscale) – the values assigned to each pixel

Together, these elements form any digital image.

docs.title: Pixels: The Building Blocks
docs.text:
A pixel is the smallest unit of a digital image.

You can think of it as:

  • a tiny square
  • containing a single colour value

When many pixels are placed together in a grid, they form shapes and images. The total number of pixels used in an image is called its:

resolution

More pixels generally mean:

  • more detail
  • smoother images

docs.title: Two Ways to Build Images
docs.text:
There are two main approaches to creating images in 2D digital art:

  • Bitmap (Raster) images
  • Vector images

Both use completely different methods to represent visual information.

docs.title: Bitmap (Raster) Images
docs.text:
Bitmap images are the most straightforward type.

They work like a grid:

  • imagine a large empty matrix (or raster)
  • each position in that grid holds one pixel
  • each pixel gets a colour value

You can compare this to a mosaic or something like Ministeck:

  • each piece represents a pixel
  • placing coloured pieces in the right positions creates an image

A bitmap image is created by assigning a colour to every individual pixel in a grid

This method allows for:

  • complex detail
  • soft shading
  • painterly effects

But it also means:

  • large file sizes
  • resolution limitations when scaling

docs.title: Vector Images
docs.text:
Vector images work in a completely different way.

Instead of filling a grid with pixels, they use:

  • points (coordinates)
  • lines between those points

A helpful comparison is a connect-the-dots puzzle:

  • points are placed on a coordinate system (X and Y)
  • the computer connects these points with lines
  • shapes are formed and then filled with colour

These lines are called vectors.

A vector image is defined by mathematical relationships, not individual pixels

docs.title: Comparing Bitmap and Vector Images
docs.text:
Both systems have strengths and weaknesses.

Vector Images

  • Made of lines and shapes
  • Clean and sharp appearance
  • Often use flat colours or simple gradients
  • Very small file sizes
  • Can scale infinitely without losing quality

Bitmap Images

  • Made of individual pixels
  • More detailed and expressive
  • Better for textures and painting
  • Larger file sizes
  • Lose quality when scaled up

docs.title: Colour in Both Systems
docs.text:
There is also a difference in how colour is used:

  • Bitmap images
  • each pixel has its own colour value
  • allows for subtle variations and shading
  • Vector images
  • colour is usually assigned to whole shapes
  • often uniform or gradient-based

This difference strongly influences the visual style:

  • bitmap → painterly, organic
  • vector → clean, graphic

docs.title: Why Both Matter in Animation
docs.text:
Both bitmap and vector techniques are widely used in animation:

  • Bitmap animation
  • hand-drawn styles
  • detailed textures
  • natural shading
  • Vector animation
  • clean shapes
  • scalable graphics
  • efficient production

Understanding the difference helps you choose the right approach for your project.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• A digital image is data that represents visual information
  • It requires:
  • pixels
  • structure
  • colour
  • Bitmap images use a grid of pixels
  • Vector images use points and mathematical lines
  • Each method has its own strengths and applications

Coming Up Next: Pixels

In the next chapter, we take a closer look at the smallest building block of all digital images: the pixel.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Right now, you are looking at millions of tiny elements on your screen.

Most screens today display images using millions of pixels. For example, a Full HD (1920 × 1080) screen contains about 2 million pixels, while a 4K screen contains over 8 million, and an 8K display more than 33 million. The higher the resolution, the more detail an image can show. Together, these pixels form the images and animations you see every day.

Understanding how pixels actually work is essential for digital animation.

In this chapter, we explore what pixels are, how they form images, and why their structure matters.

docs.title: What Is a Pixel?
docs.text:
A digital image is often stored as a bitmap.

A bitmap is simply a grid (or matrix) filled with small coloured squares. Each of these squares is called a pixel (short for picture element).

You can think of an image as:

  • a grid
  • filled with pixels
  • where each pixel has a colour value

3.3 The Simplest Pixel: Black and White (1-bit)

The most basic type of pixel can only have two states:

  • black
  • white

There are no shades of gray—just on or off.

This works like a light switch:

  • off → black (0)
  • on → white (1)

Because there are only two values (0 and 1), this is called a 1-bit image.

docs.title: Example: Drawing with Pixels
docs.text:
Imagine creating a simple image—like an eye—using only black and white pixels.

By turning individual pixels on and off, an image begins to appear.

If we represent this with numbers, the computer does not “see” an eye at all. It only sees something like this:

000000011111111000000

000000111111111110000

000011111111110111000

000010011110110001100

001100011111110000110

011000011111110000011

110000001111100000001

To us, this might look like a shape or even an eye (especially if you squint). But to the computer, it is simply data: zeros and ones.

docs.title: From Pixels to an Image
docs.text:
By arranging pixels, we create images.

By placing pixels in sequence, we an image.

docs.title: Resolution: How Many Pixels?
docs.text:
The quality of an image is influenced by how many pixels it contains.

This is called resolution.

Let’s look at a simple example:

  • A small image might be 7 pixels high and 20 pixels wide
  • If we reduce it further, the image becomes harder to recognize
  • If we increase it—for example to 60 × 100 pixels—the image becomes clearer

The more pixels you use, the more detail you can display.

Higher pixel count = higher resolution = more detail

docs.title: Resolution vs. Detail
docs.text:
Resolution refers to the number of pixels in an image, not necessarily how good the image is.

However:

  • more pixels allow for more detail
  • fewer pixels limit what can be shown

You can think of resolution as: The density of the pixel grid

docs.title: Bit Depth (Preview)
docs.text:
Earlier, we used a 1-bit image, where each pixel could only be black or white.

But most images use more than two values per pixel.

The number of possible values a pixel can store is called bit depth.

  • 1-bit → 2 values (black and white)
  • more bits → more possible colours or shades

Bit depth determines how smooth gradients and colours appear.

This will be explored further in the next chapter.

docs.title: Pixels and Colour (Looking Ahead)
docs.text:
So far, we have only worked with black and white pixels.

Modern screens display millions of colours, but they still use the same basic principle:

  • each pixel stores data
  • that data defines its colour

In the next chapter, we will explore:

  • how colour is stored
  • how screens display colour
  • and how digital colour systems work

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• A pixel is the smallest unit of a digital image
  • A bitmap is a grid of pixels
  • A 1-bit image uses only black and white (0 and 1)
  • Computers store images as numerical data, not visual shapes
  • Resolution is the number of pixels in an image
  • More pixels result in more detail

Explore It Yourself

If you want to experiment with pixels yourself, you can try simple pixel art tools such as:

  • https://make8bitart.com/
  • https://www.piskelapp.com/

Coming Up Next

Chapter 4 – Bit Depth and grayscale

In the next chapter, we will explore how pixels store grayscale and how digital images move beyond simple black and white.

docs.title: From Black & White to Grayscale
docs.text:
In the previous chapter, we looked at the simplest possible image: a 1-bit image, where each pixel can only be black or white.

While this is enough to create basic images, it is very limited. Most animations and images require more subtlety—variations between black and white.

To achieve this, we introduce grayscale.

Instead of just two values black (0) and white (1) we now create many intermediate steps between them.

docs.title: Bits and Counting
docs.text:
To understand grayscale, we need to understand how computers count.

A computer works with bits, where each bit can be:

  • 0 (off)
  • 1 (on)

With more bits, we can store higher numbers.

Counting with Bits

  • 1 bit → can count 0–1 (2 values)
  • 2 bits → can count 0–3 (4 values)
  • 3 bits → can count 0–7 (8 values)

Each additional bit doubles the number of possible values.

This follows a pattern based on powers of two (2N):

1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128...

docs.title: The 8-Bit System (A Byte)
docs.text:
When we combine 8 bits, we get a byte.

An 8-bit system can represent:

0 → 255

That means:

  • 256 possible values in total

This is the standard for many images.

Understanding the Value System

Each bit represents a value:

1 2 4 8 16 32 64 128

By switching bits on or off and adding their values together, the computer creates numbers.

For example:

  • only first bit on → 1
  • second bit on → 2
  • both on → 3
  • all on → 255

docs.title: Grayscale in 8 Bits
docs.text:
Now we apply this to images.

In an 8-bit grayscale image:

  • 0 = black
  • 255 = white
  • everything in between = shades of gray

This gives us:

256 steps from black to white

For example:

  • 0 → black
  • 128 → roughly mid-gray
  • 255 → white

These intermediate values create smooth transitions and allow for more detail.

docs.title: Banding
docs.text:
An 8-bit gradient often looks smooth at first glance.

However, when you zoom in or push the image too far, you may start to see visible steps between shades.

This effect is called banding.

Banding happens because:

  • there are still only 256 steps
  • the transition is not truly continuous

docs.title: Increasing Bit Depth
docs.text:
To reduce banding and increase detail, we can increase the bit depth.

16-Bit Images

  • Range: 0 → 65,535
  • Much smoother gradients
  • Banding becomes almost invisible

32-Bit Images

  • Extremely high precision
  • Often used in professional workflows (especially compositing and rendering)

Why Higher Bit Depth Matters

Higher bit depth allows:

  • smoother gradients
  • more precise colour control
  • better results in editing and compositing

For example:

  • an 8-bit gradient may show visible steps
  • a 16-bit gradient appears almost perfectly smooth

However, there is a trade-off:

  • higher bit depth significantly increases file size
  • it also requires more processing power and memory

Higher bit depth provides better image quality, but it comes at a cost: files become larger, and performance can decrease, especially in complex projects.

docs.title: Bit Depth vs Computer Architecture
docs.text:
You may have heard of:

  • 16-bit computers
  • 32-bit systems
  • 64-bit processors

These refer to how computers process data, not directly to image colour.

However, they are related in concept: both use powers of two.

Common values you will encounter include:

8 – 16 – 32 – 64 – 128 – 256 – 512 – 1024 – 2048 – 4096 – 8192

These numbers are fundamental in digital technology but aren’t very user-friendly. That’s why bigger numbers are normalised.

4.8 Normalized Values (0–1 Range)

Many modern programs do not display grayscale values as 0–255 or 0–65,535.

Instead, they normalize the range to:

0.0 → 1.0

This means:

  • → black
  • 0.5 → mid-gray
  • → white

Why Normalize?

Normalization has several advantages:

  • Simpler calculations No need to work with large numbers
  • Consistency across bit depths
  • 0.5 is always mid-gray
  • works the same in 8-bit and 16-bit
  • Better for combining images Especially useful in compositing and effects

docs.title: Applying This to Images
docs.text:
If we return to the pixel examples from the previous chapter:

  • A 1-bit image gives a rough shape
  • An 8-bit grayscale image adds shading and depth
  • A 16-bit image improves smoothness and detail even furtherThe more levels you have between black and white, the more realistic and nuanced your image becomes.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Bit depth determines how many values a pixel can store
  • 8 bits (1 byte) = 256 levels (0–255)
  • 16 bits = 65,536 levels
  • More bit depth = smoother gradients and more detail
  • Banding occurs when there are too few steps
  • Many programs use normalized values (0–1)

Coming Up Next

Chapter 5 – 1. Image Resolution and Aspect Ratio In the next chapter, we will take a closer look at what resolution really means, and how different screen sizes and formats are used in practice.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
In the chapter about pixels, we explored how images are built from small units and how bit depth determines the number of colour or grayscale values.

Now we shift to another meaning of the word resolution:

not how many shades a pixel can store (bit depth), but how many pixels make up the image itself.

This is called image resolution.

docs.title: Resolution as Image Size
docs.text:
Image resolution refers to:

the total number of pixels in an image

It is usually written as:

width × height (in pixels)

For example:

  • 1920 × 1080
  • 3840 × 2160
  • 7680 × 4320

Important Distinction: Size vs Display

It is important not to confuse image resolution with physical size.

  • A 10 × 10 pixel image can be projected onto a skyscraper
  • An 8K image can be displayed on a tiny screen

In both cases:

the number of pixels remains exactly the same

docs.title: About PPI and DPI
docs.text:
You may have heard terms like:

  • PPI (pixels per inch)
  • DPI (dots per inch)

These measure how densely pixels are packed on a surface.

However, in digital animation:

PPI and DPI are mostly irrelevant

Why? Because animation is designed for screens, where:

  • the pixel count matters
  • not the physical size

docs.title: Common Screen Resolutions
docs.text:
Different platforms use different standard resolutions.

Common Formats

  • HD (720p) → 1280 × 720 (basic / legacy standard)
  • Full HD (1080p) → 1920 × 1080 (still widely used)
  • QHD / 1440p → 2560 × 1440 (common for monitors and gaming)
  • 2K (DCI) → 2048 × 1080 (cinema standard)
  • UHD / 4K (TV) → 3840 × 2160 (current industry standard)
  • DCI 4K (Cinema) → 4096 × 2160 (cinema production)
  • 8K → 7680 × 4320 (high-end / emerging)

Why So Many Standards?

Different industries use slightly different formats.

These standards are defined by organizations such as:

SMPTE (Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers)

They establish technical agreements for:

  • film
  • television
  • digital cinema

A Common Source of Confusion: 4K

There are two common “4K” resolutions:

  • TV / UHD 4K → 3840 × 2160 (aspect ratio 16:9)
  • Cinema (DCI 4K) → 4096 × 2160 (slightly wider)

Same name—different dimensions.

docs.title: Aspect Ratio
docs.text:
Resolution tells us how many pixels there are.

Aspect ratio tells us:

the relationship between width and height

Calculating Aspect Ratio:

Aspect ratio is calculated by dividing: width ÷ height

Examples:

Full HD (1920 × 1080):

  • 1920 ÷ 1080 ≈ 1.78
  • written as → 16:9

2K (2048 × 1080):

  • 2048 ÷ 1080 ≈ 1.90
  • written as → 1.9:1

Different Notations

Aspect ratios can be written in two ways:

  • Film notation → 1.85:1, 2.39:1
  • TV notation → 16:9

Both describe the same idea, just formatted differently.

docs.title: Common Aspect Ratios
docs.text:
Some widely used aspect ratios include:

    • 16:9 → standard for television and online video
  • 1.85:1 → common cinema widescreen
  • 2.39:1 → cinematic “ultrawide” (CinemaScope)
  • 1.9:1 → digital cinema standard (DCI formats)
  • 9:16 → vertical video (mobile devices, social media)
  • 4:3 → older television format
  • 21:9 → ultrawide monitors and cinematic displays

docs.title: Why Aspect Ratio Matters
docs.text:
The aspect ratio determines:

  • composition
  • framing
  • how your animation will appear on screen

If the aspect ratio does not match the display:

  • black bars may appear
  • or the image may be cropped or stretched

docs.title: Choosing the Right Resolution
docs.text:
In animation production, the most important question is:

Where will the animation be viewed?

Examples:

  • Online / streaming → ranges from 720p to 4K and beyond, depending on platform and bandwidth
  • Television → mainly 4K today, although much content is still produced or broadcast in 1080p
  • Cinema → 2K and 4K (DCI standards), with 4K becoming more common in modern workflows

docs.title: Resolution vs Bit Depth (Recap)
docs.text:
To avoid confusion:

  • Bit depth
  • controls colour precision
  • (example: 8-bit, 16-bit)
  • Resolution
  • controls image size in pixels
  • (example: 1920 × 1080)

Both are important, but they describe different things.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Resolution = number of pixels in an image
  • Written as width × height
  • Physical size is independent of resolution
  • PPI/DPI are not relevant for animation workflows
  • Aspect ratio describes width-to-height proportions
  • Common formats include:
  • 16:9 (TV and online)
  • 1.85:1 (cinema)
  • Always choose resolution based on final output

Coming Up Next: Chapter 5 – Digital Colour (RGB)

Now that we understand image size and structure, we return to colour and explore how pixels combine to form full-colour images.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
There are multiple ways to represent colour in digital imaging.

Since this series focuses on digital animation, we start with the most common system used in screens: the RGB colour model.

Most displays we use every day—phones, tablets, computers, and televisions—create images using this principle.

docs.title: The RGB Principle
docs.text:
RGB stands for:

  • Red
  • Green
  • Blue

In an RGB system, each pixel is made up of three smaller components, one for each of these colours.

If you zoom in far enough on a screen, you will see something like this:

tiny red, green, and blue elements grouped together

From a normal viewing distance, these separate colours blend together and form a single visible colour. When red, green, and blue are all at full intensity → the pixel appears white.

docs.title: Additive Colour
docs.text:
RGB is an additive colour model.

This means:

colours are created by adding light together

  • Red + Green + Blue → White
  • No light → Black

This is different from physical paint or ink, where mixing colours usually makes them darker.

A useful way to think about RGB is:

light is built up from darkness to brightness

docs.title: Pixels as Colour Controllers
docs.text:
Each pixel on a screen does not just store “a colour.” Instead, it contains:

  • a red value
  • a green value
  • a blue value

You can think of each of these as a separate “dimmer switch”:

  • increasing the value makes the colour brighter
  • decreasing the value makes it darker

Together, these three values define the final colour of the pixel.

docs.title: RGB as Grayscale Layers
docs.text:
A useful way to understand RGB is to think of it as:

three grayscale images stacked on top of each other

  • one grayscale image controls red intensity
  • one controls green intensity
  • one controls blue intensity

Each of these is called a channel.

So instead of one grayscale image, an RGB image contains three channels (R, G, B)

When combined, these channels create a full-colour image.

docs.title: Image Settings in Practice
docs.text:
When creating a new image in software such as Krita, you will see settings like:

  • Width → number of horizontal pixels
  • Height → number of vertical pixels
  • Colour Model → usually set to RGB
  • Alpha → transparency (explained later)

About Resolution (Important Clarification)

You may see settings like “72 pixels per inch (ppi)”, but for screen-based work this value is often misunderstood.

For digital displays:

resolution is determined by pixel dimensions (width × height), not pixels per inch

For example:

1920 × 1080 pixels looks the same image-wise

  • on a phone
  • on a monitor
  • on a cinema screen

Only the physical size changes, not the number of pixels.

docs.title: From Grayscale to Colour
docs.text:
In the previous chapter, we explored grayscale images.

Now we expand that idea:

  • each channel (R, G, B) is itself a grayscale image
  • each pixel stores a grayscale value per channel

For example (8-bit per channel):

  • Red = 255
  • Green = 0
  • Blue = 0

→ the result is pure red

Another example:

  • Red = 255
  • Green = 255
  • Blue = 255

→ the result is white

docs.title: Bit Depth in Colour Images
docs.text:
In most common RGB images:

  • each channel uses 8 bits
  • that gives 256 values per channel

Since there are 3 channels:

256 × 256 × 256 = 16.7 million possible colours

This is why 8-bit RGB images are often called “24-bit colour” (8 bits × 3 channels).

Higher bit depths (like 16-bit per channel) allow for:

  • smoother gradients
  • more accurate colour representation
  • better editing results

REDGREENBLUE

docs.title: Alpha Channel (Transparency)
docs.text:
In addition to Red, Green, and Blue, many images include a fourth channel:

  • Alpha

The alpha channel controls transparency:

  • 0 → fully transparent
  • 1 (or max value) → fully visible

This is essential in animation for:

  • layering images
  • compositing
  • visual effects

Transparency will be explored further in a later chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Most screens use the RGB colour model
  • Each pixel consists of three components: red, green, blue
  • RGB is an additive system (adding light creates white)
  • An RGB image consists of three grayscale channels
  • Combined channels produce full colour
  • Standard images use 8 bits per channel (24-bit colour total)
  • Resolution for screens is defined by pixel dimensions, not ppi

Coming Up Next: Chapter 6 – Colour Spaces and Colour Management

In the next chapter, we take a deeper look at how colour is organized, stored, and interpreted inside digital systems.

docs.title: What Is Transparency
docs.text:
So far, we have looked at how images are built using pixels, resolution, and colour. Each pixel stores colour information using RGB values.

However, there is another important aspect of an image:

whether a pixel is visible or not

It’s a channel just like RGB but it determines if whether a pixel is visible or not. Transparency determines:

  • which pixels of an image are visible
  • which pixels are hidden
  • and how transparent or opaque an pixel is

For example:

  • a character can be drawn without a background
  • allowing it to be placed on top of another image

Without transparency, every image would always be a solid rectangle.

docs.title: The Alpha Channel
docs.text:
Transparency is stored in an additional channel called the alpha channel.

Just like RGB:

  • red → one channel
  • green → one channel
  • blue → one channel

Alpha is:

  • one extra channel
  • storing visibility instead of colour

This channel is often called A. So we have RGBA if we need transparency.

How Alpha Works

The alpha channel is a greyscale image:

  • 0 → fully transparent (invisible)
  • 0.5 → semi-transparent
  • 1 → fully opaque (visible)

This is the same idea as bit depth and greyscale values discussed earlier.

The alpha channel controls how much of a pixel you can see.

RGBA Images

When RGB and Alpha are combined, we get:

RGBA = Red, Green, Blue, Alpha

This means each pixel contains:

  • colour information
  • visibility information

docs.title: Layers and Image Stacking
docs.text:
Transparency allows images to be placed on top of each other.

This is commonly referred to as layers.

  • background layer → sits at the bottom
  • foreground layer → sits on top
  • each layer can have transparency

How Layers Work

When stacking images:

  • visible pixels cover what is below
  • transparent pixels reveal what is underneath

This makes it possible to:

  • combine drawings
  • add effects
  • build complex images step by step

docs.title: Basic Compositing
docs.text:
The process of combining images using transparency is called:

compositing

In compositing:

  • multiple images are layered
  • the alpha channel determines how they interact

Simple Example

Imagine:

  • a character on a transparent background
  • placed on top of a landscape

The result:

  • the character appears inside the scene
  • without any visible rectangle around it

This is possible because:

the transparent pixels allow the background to show through

Why Compositing Matters

Compositing is used in:

  • animation
  • visual effects
  • motion graphics

It allows different elements to be:

  • created separately
  • and combined into one final image

docs.title: Common Problems
docs.text:
Working with transparency can introduce issues.

Edge Halos

Sometimes you may see:

  • light or dark edges around objects

This happens when:

  • background colour information is mixed into the edges

Premultiplied vs Straight Alpha

There are two ways alpha can be stored Straight (unassociated) alpha and Premultiplied alpha.

Straight Alpha:

  • RGB stores original colour values
  • alpha is applied separately

Premultiplied Alpha:

  • RGB values are already multiplied by alpha
  • useful for rendering and compositing

Why This Matters

If these types are mixed incorrectly:

  • edges may appear wrong
  • transparency may not behave as expected

docs.title: Practical Examples
docs.text:
In practice, transparency is used in many everyday situations.

Example 1 – Character Animation

  • characters are drawn without backgrounds
  • placed over scenes

Example 2 – Visual Effects

  • smoke, fire, or light effects
  • layered on top of footage

Example 3 – User Interface Design

  • icons with transparent backgrounds
  • used across different layouts

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Transparency controls whether a pixel is visible
  • The alpha channel stores transparency as greyscale
  • RGBA images combine colour and visibility
  • Layers allow images to be stacked
  • Compositing combines images into a final result
  • Incorrect handling can cause visual artefacts

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
In the previous chapter, we explored RGB colour and bit depth—how software represents and calculates colour.

However, there is another important layer to understand:

how colour is actually displayed on different devices

What you see on your screen is not only determined by software, but also by hardware, such as:

  • monitors
  • televisions
  • projectors
  • cinema screens

And here’s the challenge:

No two screens display colour exactly the same.

docs.title: The Problem: Devices Show Colour Differently
docs.text:
Different displays can vary in:

  • brightness
  • contrast
  • colour saturation
  • colour accuracy

Even two screens of the same model can look slightly different.

This means:

  • the colours you create on one device
  • may look different on another

For example:

  • an image that looks bright on your laptop
  • might appear darker on another screen or vice versa

docs.title: The Solution: Colour Spaces and ICC Profiles
docs.text:
To solve this problem, the industry introduced standardized systems for handling colour.

One of the most important concepts is Colour Spaces

A colour space defines a range of colours that can be represented.

You can think of it as: a shared “language” for colour

ICC Profiles

An ICC profile (International Colour Consortium profile) describes how a specific device handles colour.

It acts as a translator:

  • it converts colours from a device-specific format
  • into a common colour space
  • and back again for another device

So the workflow becomes:

Device A → ICC profile → common colour space → ICC profile → Device B

This ensures that colours remain as consistent as possible across different screens.

docs.title: Colour Gamut
docs.text:
Each device can only display a limited range of colours.

This range is called the:

colour gamut

  • some devices show a small range of colours
  • others can display wider, more saturated colours

docs.title: The CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram
docs.text:
To visualize colour gamut’s, we use a standard model:

CIE 1931 Colour Model

This model maps colours visible to the human eye.

  • it represents colours based on wavelengths (nanometers)
  • usually shown as a 2D diagram (chromaticity diagram)
  • although real colour perception is three-dimensional

This diagram is not perfect, but it provides a useful reference for comparing colour spaces.

docs.title: Common Colour Spaces
docs.text:
Different industries use different standard colour spaces.

sRGB

  • Most common colour space for:
  • screens
  • web content
  • Designed as a standard for consistency
  • Relatively limited colour range

Adobe RGB

  • Wider colour gamut than sRGB
  • Designed to include more colours used in printing (CMYK)
  • Better for professional image work

Even so:

it still covers only a portion of all visible colours

Beyond sRGB and Adobe RGB

As technology improves, newer displays support:

  • wide colour gamuts
  • more accurate colour reproduction

These are sometimes visualized using extended versions of colour models, such as:

  • CIExyY (which also includes brightness)

For now, understanding the basic CIE 1931 diagram is sufficient.

docs.title: Comparing Gamut’s
docs.text:
When different colour spaces are plotted on the same diagram:

  • sRGB covers a certain area
  • Adobe RGB covers a larger area
  • modern displays may go even beyond

This shows:

not every device can display every colour

docs.title: Why This Matters in Animation
docs.text:
When working in animation:

  • you might create an image in one colour space
  • view it on another device
  • export it to a different format

Without proper colour management:

  • colours can shift
  • images can look inconsistent

Understanding colour spaces helps you:

  • maintain consistent visuals
  • avoid unexpected colour changes
  • prepare work for different outputs (screen, web, print)

docs.title: A Note on Calibration
docs.text:
Even with colour spaces and ICC profiles, screens can still be inaccurate.

This is why professionals use:

calibration

Calibration adjusts a display so it matches a known standard.

This topic will be explored further in a later chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Different devices display colour differently
  • A colour space defines a shared range of colours
  • An ICC profile translates colour between devices
  • A colour gamut is the range of colours a device can display
  • The CIE 1931 model is used to visualize colour spaces
  • sRGB and Adobe RGB are common standards
  • No device can display the full range of visible colours

Coming Up Next

Chapter 10 – Colour Management in Practice

In the next chapter, we will look at how to work with colour spaces, ICC profiles, and calibration in a real animation workflow.

docs.title: Colour Management in Practice
docs.text:
Consistent Colour Starts with Your Screen

In the previous chapter, we introduced colour spaces as a way to standardise colour across different devices. A colour space defines which colours can be represented.

However, this system only works if your display actually shows those colours correctly.

A colour space is a standard—but your monitor is not.

Every display:

  • is manufactured differently
  • ages over time
  • has its own brightness, contrast, and colour response

Even two identical monitors can show slightly different colours.

The Problem Without Calibration

If your monitor is not calibrated, the colours you see are not reliable.

For example:

  • colours may appear too warm or too cool
  • shadows may look too dark or too bright
  • saturation may be exaggerated or muted

This leads to a critical issue:

You are making decisions based on incorrect visual information.

In practice, this means:

  • your animation looks different on another properly calibrated screen
  • colours shift when viewed on a phone, TV, or projector
  • collaboration becomes unreliable

Calibration as a Reference Point

Monitor calibration solves this problem by bringing your display to a known standard.

Calibration adjusts your monitor so that it matches common reference values such as:

  • White point (D65 / 6500K)
  • Gamma (usually 2.2)
  • Brightness (around 120 cd/m² for editing)

These values are not arbitrary—they are part of the same standardised system used in colour spaces.

Link to Colour Spaces (Key Concept)

Colour spaces (such as sRGB or DCI-P3) define:

which colours should be displayed

Calibration ensures:

your monitor displays those colours correctly

Without calibration:

  • the colour space is correct
  • but your display interpretation is wrong

With calibration:

  • your display becomes a reliable reference

The Role of ICC Profiles

After calibration, your system creates or applies an ICC profile.

An ICC profile describes:

  • how your specific monitor behaves
  • how it differs from an ideal standard

Software uses this profile to:

  • correct colour output
  • translate between devices

In this way, the ICC profile acts as a bridge between your monitor and the colour space.

Why This Matters in Practice

In a real production pipeline:

  • your work is viewed on multiple devices
  • each device interprets colour slightly differently

Calibration + ICC profiles help ensure that:

  • colours remain consistent
  • creative decisions remain valid
  • the final result matches your intention

Without calibration:

  • colour becomes unpredictable
  • workflows break down
  • results become unreliable

A Practical Perspective

For student-level work:

  • calibration may not always be perfect
  • default settings may still be used

However:

Even a basic calibration improves consistency significantly.

In professional environments:

  • calibration is standard practice
  • displays are regularly checked and recalibrated

Summary

  • Colour spaces define how colours should behave
  • Monitors interpret those colours differently
  • Calibration aligns your monitor with a known standard
  • ICC profiles translate between devices
  • Together, they make colour consistent and predictable

docs.title: 260702   INTRO BOOK
docs.text:
Digital Imaging – Technical Foundations

An Online Book about Digital images – Technical Foundations

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this series on the technical side of digital animation.

There is no shortage of tutorials online that show you how to make animations. Animators explain software, workflows, and tricks. You can find countless videos demonstrating tips, techniques, and shortcuts.

However, if you want to understand what actually happens under the hood—what occurs when you apply a filter, choose a colour, or render an image—you’ll quickly find that this type of knowledge is much harder to access. The information exists, but it is scattered across technical articles and documentation. Finding it often feels like searching for a needle in a haystack.

This book series aims to bring that knowledge together.

Rather than focusing on specific software, we will explore the underlying principles of digital animation. The goal is to provide a fundamental understanding that applies across all tools and platforms. Whether you use Blender, Maya, After Effects, or any other software, the concepts remain the same.

These materials are originally developed for my students at the art academy St. Joost in the south of the Netherlands, but they are intended for anyone interested in understanding animation beyond the interface.

Throughout this series, examples will often be demonstrated using open-source software, making the material accessible to everyone.

docs.title: Types of Digital Animation
docs.text: The term digital animation covers a wide range of techniques. Each type has its own workflow, tools, and required skill set. To understand the technical side of animation, it is important to first distinguish between the main forms.

docs.title: 1.1 Hand-Drawn Animation (2D Animation)
docs.text:
Hand-drawn animation—also known as traditional or cel animation—is one of the oldest forms of animation.

At its core, this technique involves drawing each frame individually. Historically, these drawings were created on paper or transparent acetate sheets (called cels), which is where the term cel animation originates.

While the tools have evolved, the principle remains the same.

  • In the early days, animators worked entirely by hand using paper and paint.
  • In the 1990s, drawings were often created on paper but then scanned and coloured digitally.
  • Today, most hand-drawn animation is produced directly on computers using drawing tablets.

Despite this technological shift, the defining feature remains: every frame is crafted manually, following the same principles developed during the early decades of animation.

Hand-drawn animation can therefore exist in three forms:

  • fully analog
  • fully digital
  • or a hybrid of both

docs.title: 1.2 Stop Motion Animation
docs.text:
Stop motion animation works fundamentally differently. Instead of drawing frames, it involves manipulating real-world objects.

The process is simple in principle:

  1. Move an object slightly
  2. Take a photograph
  3. Repeat

When these images are played in sequence, they create the illusion of motion.

Stop motion is incredibly versatile. It can be created using:

  • everyday objects
  • puppets
  • clay
  • large-scale constructions
  • or even microscopic materials

Common Forms of Stop Motion

Puppet Animation Most professional stop-motion films use puppets built around an internal structure called an armature. This acts as a skeleton, allowing the figure to hold poses and remain stable during animation.

Claymation In this form, characters and environments are made from malleable materials such as plasticine. Because the material can be reshaped, it allows for expressive and organic motion.

Cut-Out Animation (2D Stop Motion) This technique uses flat, pre-cut shapes that are moved frame by frame and typically filmed from above. It bridges the gap between 2D animation and physical stop motion.

docs.title: 1.3 Computer-Generated Animation (3D / CG Animation)
docs.text:
Computer-generated animation (CG or CGI) is the most recent major development in animation.

Unlike hand-drawn animation, CG animation is not created by drawing each frame. Instead, it involves building digital objects and controlling them within a virtual environment.

A useful way to think about 3D animation is:

It is closer to puppetry than drawing.

How It Works

  1. Modeling – Characters and objects are created digitally
  2. Rigging – A virtual skeleton is added to control movement
  3. Animation – The animator sets key poses (keyframes)
  4. Interpolation – The computer calculates the motion between these poses

This process makes CG animation highly controllable and flexible.

Relation to Other Techniques

  • Like stop motion, it involves posing objects in space
  • Like hand-drawn animation, it still relies on frame sequences
  • But it differs in that the computer automates parts of the process

docs.title: 1.4 What All Animation Has in Common
docs.text:
Despite their differences, all animation techniques share a fundamental characteristic:

They are ultimately displayed as a sequence of images on a screen.

Today, these images are almost always digital. That means they are made up of pixels.

Understanding pixels—and how digital images work—is essential for understanding modern animation. This will be explored in detail in a later chapter.

Coming Up Next

Before diving into digital image theory, we will first take a closer look at the tools used in animation:

docs.title: Software and Workflow in Digital Animation
2.1 Introduction
docs.text:
Creating animation requires more than just animation software.

Before anything starts moving, ideas must be developed, written, and visualized. A script needs to be written, a storyboard must be created, and designs have to be developed. At the same time, the entire process must be carefully planned and organized.

This complete system—where all parts of production work together—is called the pipeline.

In general, animation production is divided into three main phases:

  • Preproduction – concept, writing, and design
  • Production – creating the animation
  • Postproduction – editing, compositing, and finishing

In this chapter, we will look at the software used throughout these phases, with a focus on accessible and open-source tools.

docs.title: 2.2 Open-Source Software
docs.text:
Open-source software is released under a license that makes its source code publicly available.

In practice, this means:

  • it is free to use
  • it is often developed collaboratively
  • and it can compete with professional commercial software

For students and independent creators, open-source tools provide a strong and flexible starting point.

docs.title: 2.3 Software Categories in the Animation Pipeline
docs.text:
Before looking at individual programs, it is useful to understand that animation software generally falls into a few key categories:

  • Writing and planning tools
  • Design and image creation tools
  • Animation tools
  • Compositing and editing tools
  • Sound tools

Understanding these categories is more important than any specific program. Software changes over time, but the pipeline remains largely the same.

docs.title: Scriptwriting
docs.text:
Professional studios often use tools such as:

  • Final Draft
  • Celtx
  • StudioBinder

However, these can be expensive. Good free alternatives include:

  • Trelby
  • Fountain
  • Scrite

Storytelling itself falls outside the scope of this series, but there are many excellent resources available. A recommended starting point is Animated Storytelling by Liz Blazer.

docs.title: Drawing and Sketching
docs.text:
Early ideas are often developed through sketches. This can be done on paper, which is still a very strong starting point.

For digital work, the following tools are excellent:

  • Krita – powerful and widely used for digital painting and drawing
  • GIMP – best suited for image manipulation and photo editing
  • Affinity designer (not open source but still free)

Commercial tools like Adobe Photoshop are widely used in the industry, but open-source alternatives are more than capable. If you want to work in the industry it can be wise to invest in adobe products.

docs.title: Graphic Design (Vector Graphics)
docs.text:
For clean lines and scalable graphics, vector-based tools are used.

  • Inkscape – the main open-source alternative to Adobe Illustrator

This is especially useful for:

  • logos
  • stylized artwork
  • technical or graphic animation styles

Commercial tools like Adobe Illustrator are widely used in the industry.

docs.title: 3D Sculpting (Design Phase)
docs.text:
For artists who prefer shaping rather than drawing, digital sculpting offers a more tactile workflow.

  • Industry standard: ZBrush
  • Best open-source solution: Blender (sculpt mode)

Smaller tools like SculptGL exist, but Blender has largely replaced them in most workflows.

docs.title: Storyboarding and Animatics
docs.text:
Once writing and design are in place, the next step is to assemble the story visually.

Storyboarding software helps with:

  • organizing panels
  • adjusting timing
  • rearranging shots

Recommended tools:

  • Storyboarder (open-source)
  • Toon Boom Storyboard Pro (industry standard)

These tools can export sequences to create an animatic, a rough version of the final film that includes timing and sometimes basic sound.

docs.title: Sound (Pre- and postproduction Stage)
docs.text:
Sound work often begins during the animatic phase.

Typical tasks:

  • recording dialogue
  • placing temporary sound effects
  • rough sound editing
  • Audacity is the most widely used open-source audio editor for this purpose

docs.title: Hand-Drawn (2D) Animation
docs.text:
Beginner-friendly tools:

  • Pencil2D
  • Krita

More advanced options:

  • Tahoma2D / OpenToonz – professional-level, open-source
  • Blender (Grease Pencil) – combines 2D and 3D workflows

Professional tools include TV-Paint, Moho and Toon Boom Harmony.

docs.title: Stop Motion Animation
docs.text:
Stop-motion animation requires software to capture and organize frames.

  • Industry standard: Dragonframe

Open/free alternatives:

  • qStopMotion – the most complete open-source option
  • Stop Motion Studio (not open-source but widely used in education)

docs.title: Computer-Generated (3D / CG) Animation
docs.text:
3D animation is technically the most complex form.

Industry tools:

  • Autodesk Maya
  • Houdini
  • Cinema 4D

Blender

The most important open-source alternative is Blender, which has grown into a complete production suite.

Blender can be used for:

  • 3D modelling
  • animation
  • sculpting
  • compositing
  • video editing
  • even 2D animation

Because of this versatility, it is often described as a “Swiss army knife” of animation software.

For beginners and professionals alike, Blender is a tool you cannot ignore.

docs.title: Postproduction Software
docs.text: Postproduction is often overlooked by beginners, but it is a crucial part of the pipeline.

docs.title: Compositing
docs.text:
Compositing is the process of combining multiple image elements into a final shot.

For example:

  • combining rendered layers
  • adding effects
  • adjusting colour and lighting

Tools:

  • Blender (Compositor)
  • Natron – open-source node-based compositing (similar to Nuke)

Not open source but still free:

  • Da vinci resolve (the Fusion workspace)

docs.title: Video Editing
docs.text:
After animation and compositing, everything needs to be assembled into a final film.

Open-source editing tools:

  • Kdenlive – powerful and widely used
  • Shotcut – simpler and beginner-friendly
  • Blender (basic editing also possible)

Not open Source, but still free

  • Da vinci Resolve

Sound

Sound always starts in preproduction but is refined and finalized during postproduction.

  • dialogue is cleaned
  • sound effects are layered
  • music is added
  • Audacity can also be used in this stage

docs.title: Example Workflow (Personal Toolkit)
docs.text:
To illustrate how these tools can work together, here is an example setup:

  • Trelby – scriptwriting
  • Krita – 2D design
  • Inkscape – vector graphics
  • Storyboarder – storyboards and animatics
  • Audacity – sound editing
  • OpenToonz – 2D animation
  • qStopMotion – stop-motion animation
  • Blender – 3D animation, sculpting, compositing, and editing

For most tasks, Blender plays a central role, especially in modern digital workflows.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
If you are new to digital animation, the best approach is to keep things simple:

Start with Krita for drawing, and Blender for animation.

From there, you can gradually expand your toolkit as your projects become more complex.

Coming Up Next: In the next chapter, we move away from software and focus on the foundation of digital imagery:

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Throughout this book, we will work primarily with digital images. Before diving into technical details like pixels and colour, it is important to understand what a digital image actually is. A simple definition might be: A digital image is a visual representation built from data.To compare, Wikipedia defines an image as: “An artifact that depicts visual perception… usually resembling a physical object.”

In other words:

  • an image represents something
  • and uses visual elements—such as colour and shape—to do so

For computers, however, an image is not something visual. It is data that describes visual information. There are two main ways to represent this data. The first is the bitmap image, which is the most important to understand. No matter how an image is created, it will always be displayed on your screen as a bitmap in some form.

docs.title: Three Ingredients of a Digital Image
docs.text:
In simple terms, a computer needs three main components to create an bitmap-image:

  1. Pixels – the smallest building blocks
  2. A structure – a way to organize those pixels
  3. Colour (or grayscale) – the values assigned to each pixel

Together, these elements form any digital image.

docs.title: Pixels: The Building Blocks
docs.text:
A pixel is the smallest unit of a digital image.

You can think of it as:

  • a tiny square
  • containing a single colour value

When many pixels are placed together in a grid, they form shapes and images. The total number of pixels used in an image is called its:

resolution

More pixels generally mean:

  • more detail
  • smoother images

docs.title: Two Ways to Build Images
docs.text:
There are two main approaches to creating images in 2D digital art:

  • Bitmap (Raster) images
  • Vector images

Both use completely different methods to represent visual information.

docs.title: Bitmap (Raster) Images
docs.text:
Bitmap images are the most straightforward type.

They work like a grid:

  • imagine a large empty matrix (or raster)
  • each position in that grid holds one pixel
  • each pixel gets a colour value

You can compare this to a mosaic or something like Ministeck:

  • each piece represents a pixel
  • placing coloured pieces in the right positions creates an image

A bitmap image is created by assigning a colour to every individual pixel in a grid

This method allows for:

  • complex detail
  • soft shading
  • painterly effects

But it also means:

  • large file sizes
  • resolution limitations when scaling

docs.title: Vector Images
docs.text:
Vector images work in a completely different way.

Instead of filling a grid with pixels, they use:

  • points (coordinates)
  • lines between those points

A helpful comparison is a connect-the-dots puzzle:

  • points are placed on a coordinate system (X and Y)
  • the computer connects these points with lines
  • shapes are formed and then filled with colour

These lines are called vectors.

A vector image is defined by mathematical relationships, not individual pixels

docs.title: Comparing Bitmap and Vector Images
docs.text:
Both systems have strengths and weaknesses.

Vector Images

  • Made of lines and shapes
  • Clean and sharp appearance
  • Often use flat colours or simple gradients
  • Very small file sizes
  • Can scale infinitely without losing quality

Bitmap Images

  • Made of individual pixels
  • More detailed and expressive
  • Better for textures and painting
  • Larger file sizes
  • Lose quality when scaled up

docs.title: Colour in Both Systems
docs.text:
There is also a difference in how colour is used:

  • Bitmap images
  • each pixel has its own colour value
  • allows for subtle variations and shading
  • Vector images
  • colour is usually assigned to whole shapes
  • often uniform or gradient-based

This difference strongly influences the visual style:

  • bitmap → painterly, organic
  • vector → clean, graphic

docs.title: Why Both Matter in Animation
docs.text:
Both bitmap and vector techniques are widely used in animation:

  • Bitmap animation
  • hand-drawn styles
  • detailed textures
  • natural shading
  • Vector animation
  • clean shapes
  • scalable graphics
  • efficient production

Understanding the difference helps you choose the right approach for your project.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• A digital image is data that represents visual information
  • It requires:
  • pixels
  • structure
  • colour
  • Bitmap images use a grid of pixels
  • Vector images use points and mathematical lines
  • Each method has its own strengths and applications

Coming Up Next: Pixels

In the next chapter, we take a closer look at the smallest building block of all digital images: the pixel.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Right now, you are looking at millions of tiny elements on your screen.

Most screens today display images using millions of pixels. For example, a Full HD (1920 × 1080) screen contains about 2 million pixels, while a 4K screen contains over 8 million, and an 8K display more than 33 million. The higher the resolution, the more detail an image can show. Together, these pixels form the images and animations you see every day.

Understanding how pixels actually work is essential for digital animation.

In this chapter, we explore what pixels are, how they form images, and why their structure matters.

docs.title: What Is a Pixel?
docs.text:
A digital image is often stored as a bitmap.

A bitmap is simply a grid (or matrix) filled with small coloured squares. Each of these squares is called a pixel (short for picture element).

You can think of an image as:

  • a grid
  • filled with pixels
  • where each pixel has a colour value

3.3 The Simplest Pixel: Black and White (1-bit)

The most basic type of pixel can only have two states:

  • black
  • white

There are no shades of gray—just on or off.

This works like a light switch:

  • off → black (0)
  • on → white (1)

Because there are only two values (0 and 1), this is called a 1-bit image.

docs.title: Example: Drawing with Pixels
docs.text:
Imagine creating a simple image—like an eye—using only black and white pixels.

By turning individual pixels on and off, an image begins to appear.

If we represent this with numbers, the computer does not “see” an eye at all. It only sees something like this:

000000011111111000000

000000111111111110000

000011111111110111000

000010011110110001100

001100011111110000110

011000011111110000011

110000001111100000001

To us, this might look like a shape or even an eye (especially if you squint). But to the computer, it is simply data: zeros and ones.

docs.title: From Pixels to an Image
docs.text:
By arranging pixels, we create images.

By placing pixels in sequence, we an image.

docs.title: Resolution: How Many Pixels?
docs.text:
The quality of an image is influenced by how many pixels it contains.

This is called resolution.

Let’s look at a simple example:

  • A small image might be 7 pixels high and 20 pixels wide
  • If we reduce it further, the image becomes harder to recognize
  • If we increase it—for example to 60 × 100 pixels—the image becomes clearer

The more pixels you use, the more detail you can display.

Higher pixel count = higher resolution = more detail

docs.title: Resolution vs. Detail
docs.text:
Resolution refers to the number of pixels in an image, not necessarily how good the image is.

However:

  • more pixels allow for more detail
  • fewer pixels limit what can be shown

You can think of resolution as: The density of the pixel grid

docs.title: Bit Depth (Preview)
docs.text:
Earlier, we used a 1-bit image, where each pixel could only be black or white.

But most images use more than two values per pixel.

The number of possible values a pixel can store is called bit depth.

  • 1-bit → 2 values (black and white)
  • more bits → more possible colours or shades

Bit depth determines how smooth gradients and colours appear.

This will be explored further in the next chapter.

docs.title: Pixels and Colour (Looking Ahead)
docs.text:
So far, we have only worked with black and white pixels.

Modern screens display millions of colours, but they still use the same basic principle:

  • each pixel stores data
  • that data defines its colour

In the next chapter, we will explore:

  • how colour is stored
  • how screens display colour
  • and how digital colour systems work

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• A pixel is the smallest unit of a digital image
  • A bitmap is a grid of pixels
  • A 1-bit image uses only black and white (0 and 1)
  • Computers store images as numerical data, not visual shapes
  • Resolution is the number of pixels in an image
  • More pixels result in more detail

Explore It Yourself

If you want to experiment with pixels yourself, you can try simple pixel art tools such as:

  • https://make8bitart.com/
  • https://www.piskelapp.com/

Coming Up Next

Chapter 4 – Bit Depth and grayscale

In the next chapter, we will explore how pixels store grayscale and how digital images move beyond simple black and white.

docs.title: From Black & White to Grayscale
docs.text:
In the previous chapter, we looked at the simplest possible image: a 1-bit image, where each pixel can only be black or white.

While this is enough to create basic images, it is very limited. Most animations and images require more subtlety—variations between black and white.

To achieve this, we introduce grayscale.

Instead of just two values black (0) and white (1) we now create many intermediate steps between them.

docs.title: Bits and Counting
docs.text:
To understand grayscale, we need to understand how computers count.

A computer works with bits, where each bit can be:

  • 0 (off)
  • 1 (on)

With more bits, we can store higher numbers.

Counting with Bits

  • 1 bit → can count 0–1 (2 values)
  • 2 bits → can count 0–3 (4 values)
  • 3 bits → can count 0–7 (8 values)

Each additional bit doubles the number of possible values.

This follows a pattern based on powers of two (2N):

1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128...

docs.title: The 8-Bit System (A Byte)
docs.text:
When we combine 8 bits, we get a byte.

An 8-bit system can represent:

0 → 255

That means:

  • 256 possible values in total

This is the standard for many images.

Understanding the Value System

Each bit represents a value:

1 2 4 8 16 32 64 128

By switching bits on or off and adding their values together, the computer creates numbers.

For example:

  • only first bit on → 1
  • second bit on → 2
  • both on → 3
  • all on → 255

docs.title: Grayscale in 8 Bits
docs.text:
Now we apply this to images.

In an 8-bit grayscale image:

  • 0 = black
  • 255 = white
  • everything in between = shades of gray

This gives us:

256 steps from black to white

For example:

  • 0 → black
  • 128 → roughly mid-gray
  • 255 → white

These intermediate values create smooth transitions and allow for more detail.

docs.title: Banding
docs.text:
An 8-bit gradient often looks smooth at first glance.

However, when you zoom in or push the image too far, you may start to see visible steps between shades.

This effect is called banding.

Banding happens because:

  • there are still only 256 steps
  • the transition is not truly continuous

docs.title: Increasing Bit Depth
docs.text:
To reduce banding and increase detail, we can increase the bit depth.

16-Bit Images

  • Range: 0 → 65,535
  • Much smoother gradients
  • Banding becomes almost invisible

32-Bit Images

  • Extremely high precision
  • Often used in professional workflows (especially compositing and rendering)

Why Higher Bit Depth Matters

Higher bit depth allows:

  • smoother gradients
  • more precise colour control
  • better results in editing and compositing

For example:

  • an 8-bit gradient may show visible steps
  • a 16-bit gradient appears almost perfectly smooth

However, there is a trade-off:

  • higher bit depth significantly increases file size
  • it also requires more processing power and memory

Higher bit depth provides better image quality, but it comes at a cost: files become larger, and performance can decrease, especially in complex projects.

docs.title: Bit Depth vs Computer Architecture
docs.text:
You may have heard of:

  • 16-bit computers
  • 32-bit systems
  • 64-bit processors

These refer to how computers process data, not directly to image colour.

However, they are related in concept: both use powers of two.

Common values you will encounter include:

8 – 16 – 32 – 64 – 128 – 256 – 512 – 1024 – 2048 – 4096 – 8192

These numbers are fundamental in digital technology but aren’t very user-friendly. That’s why bigger numbers are normalised.

4.8 Normalized Values (0–1 Range)

Many modern programs do not display grayscale values as 0–255 or 0–65,535.

Instead, they normalize the range to:

0.0 → 1.0

This means:

  • → black
  • 0.5 → mid-gray
  • → white

Why Normalize?

Normalization has several advantages:

  • Simpler calculations No need to work with large numbers
  • Consistency across bit depths
  • 0.5 is always mid-gray
  • works the same in 8-bit and 16-bit
  • Better for combining images Especially useful in compositing and effects

docs.title: Applying This to Images
docs.text:
If we return to the pixel examples from the previous chapter:

  • A 1-bit image gives a rough shape
  • An 8-bit grayscale image adds shading and depth
  • A 16-bit image improves smoothness and detail even furtherThe more levels you have between black and white, the more realistic and nuanced your image becomes.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Bit depth determines how many values a pixel can store
  • 8 bits (1 byte) = 256 levels (0–255)
  • 16 bits = 65,536 levels
  • More bit depth = smoother gradients and more detail
  • Banding occurs when there are too few steps
  • Many programs use normalized values (0–1)

Coming Up Next

Chapter 5 – 1. Image Resolution and Aspect Ratio In the next chapter, we will take a closer look at what resolution really means, and how different screen sizes and formats are used in practice.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
In the chapter about pixels, we explored how images are built from small units and how bit depth determines the number of colour or grayscale values.

Now we shift to another meaning of the word resolution:

not how many shades a pixel can store (bit depth), but how many pixels make up the image itself.

This is called image resolution.

docs.title: Resolution as Image Size
docs.text:
Image resolution refers to:

the total number of pixels in an image

It is usually written as:

width × height (in pixels)

For example:

  • 1920 × 1080
  • 3840 × 2160
  • 7680 × 4320

Important Distinction: Size vs Display

It is important not to confuse image resolution with physical size.

  • A 10 × 10 pixel image can be projected onto a skyscraper
  • An 8K image can be displayed on a tiny screen

In both cases:

the number of pixels remains exactly the same

docs.title: About PPI and DPI
docs.text:
You may have heard terms like:

  • PPI (pixels per inch)
  • DPI (dots per inch)

These measure how densely pixels are packed on a surface.

However, in digital animation:

PPI and DPI are mostly irrelevant

Why? Because animation is designed for screens, where:

  • the pixel count matters
  • not the physical size

docs.title: Common Screen Resolutions
docs.text:
Different platforms use different standard resolutions.

Common Formats

  • HD (720p) → 1280 × 720 (basic / legacy standard)
  • Full HD (1080p) → 1920 × 1080 (still widely used)
  • QHD / 1440p → 2560 × 1440 (common for monitors and gaming)
  • 2K (DCI) → 2048 × 1080 (cinema standard)
  • UHD / 4K (TV) → 3840 × 2160 (current industry standard)
  • DCI 4K (Cinema) → 4096 × 2160 (cinema production)
  • 8K → 7680 × 4320 (high-end / emerging)

Why So Many Standards?

Different industries use slightly different formats.

These standards are defined by organizations such as:

SMPTE (Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers)

They establish technical agreements for:

  • film
  • television
  • digital cinema

A Common Source of Confusion: 4K

There are two common “4K” resolutions:

  • TV / UHD 4K → 3840 × 2160 (aspect ratio 16:9)
  • Cinema (DCI 4K) → 4096 × 2160 (slightly wider)

Same name—different dimensions.

docs.title: Aspect Ratio
docs.text:
Resolution tells us how many pixels there are.

Aspect ratio tells us:

the relationship between width and height

Calculating Aspect Ratio:

Aspect ratio is calculated by dividing: width ÷ height

Examples:

Full HD (1920 × 1080):

  • 1920 ÷ 1080 ≈ 1.78
  • written as → 16:9

2K (2048 × 1080):

  • 2048 ÷ 1080 ≈ 1.90
  • written as → 1.9:1

Different Notations

Aspect ratios can be written in two ways:

  • Film notation → 1.85:1, 2.39:1
  • TV notation → 16:9

Both describe the same idea, just formatted differently.

docs.title: Common Aspect Ratios
docs.text:
Some widely used aspect ratios include:

    • 16:9 → standard for television and online video
  • 1.85:1 → common cinema widescreen
  • 2.39:1 → cinematic “ultrawide” (CinemaScope)
  • 1.9:1 → digital cinema standard (DCI formats)
  • 9:16 → vertical video (mobile devices, social media)
  • 4:3 → older television format
  • 21:9 → ultrawide monitors and cinematic displays

docs.title: Why Aspect Ratio Matters
docs.text:
The aspect ratio determines:

  • composition
  • framing
  • how your animation will appear on screen

If the aspect ratio does not match the display:

  • black bars may appear
  • or the image may be cropped or stretched

docs.title: Choosing the Right Resolution
docs.text:
In animation production, the most important question is:

Where will the animation be viewed?

Examples:

  • Online / streaming → ranges from 720p to 4K and beyond, depending on platform and bandwidth
  • Television → mainly 4K today, although much content is still produced or broadcast in 1080p
  • Cinema → 2K and 4K (DCI standards), with 4K becoming more common in modern workflows

docs.title: Resolution vs Bit Depth (Recap)
docs.text:
To avoid confusion:

  • Bit depth
