  • controls colour precision
  • (example: 8-bit, 16-bit)
  • Resolution
  • controls image size in pixels
  • (example: 1920 × 1080)

Both are important, but they describe different things.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Resolution = number of pixels in an image
  • Written as width × height
  • Physical size is independent of resolution
  • PPI/DPI are not relevant for animation workflows
  • Aspect ratio describes width-to-height proportions
  • Common formats include:
  • 16:9 (TV and online)
  • 1.85:1 (cinema)
  • Always choose resolution based on final output

Coming Up Next: Chapter 5 – Digital Colour (RGB)

Now that we understand image size and structure, we return to colour and explore how pixels combine to form full-colour images.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
There are multiple ways to represent colour in digital imaging.

Since this series focuses on digital animation, we start with the most common system used in screens: the RGB colour model.

Most displays we use every day—phones, tablets, computers, and televisions—create images using this principle.

docs.title: The RGB Principle
docs.text:
RGB stands for:

  • Red
  • Green
  • Blue

In an RGB system, each pixel is made up of three smaller components, one for each of these colours.

If you zoom in far enough on a screen, you will see something like this:

tiny red, green, and blue elements grouped together

From a normal viewing distance, these separate colours blend together and form a single visible colour. When red, green, and blue are all at full intensity → the pixel appears white.

docs.title: Additive Colour
docs.text:
RGB is an additive colour model.

This means:

colours are created by adding light together

  • Red + Green + Blue → White
  • No light → Black

This is different from physical paint or ink, where mixing colours usually makes them darker.

A useful way to think about RGB is:

light is built up from darkness to brightness

docs.title: Pixels as Colour Controllers
docs.text:
Each pixel on a screen does not just store “a colour.” Instead, it contains:

  • a red value
  • a green value
  • a blue value

You can think of each of these as a separate “dimmer switch”:

  • increasing the value makes the colour brighter
  • decreasing the value makes it darker

Together, these three values define the final colour of the pixel.

docs.title: RGB as Grayscale Layers
docs.text:
A useful way to understand RGB is to think of it as:

three grayscale images stacked on top of each other

  • one grayscale image controls red intensity
  • one controls green intensity
  • one controls blue intensity

Each of these is called a channel.

So instead of one grayscale image, an RGB image contains three channels (R, G, B)

When combined, these channels create a full-colour image.

docs.title: Image Settings in Practice
docs.text:
When creating a new image in software such as Krita, you will see settings like:

  • Width → number of horizontal pixels
  • Height → number of vertical pixels
  • Colour Model → usually set to RGB
  • Alpha → transparency (explained later)

About Resolution (Important Clarification)

You may see settings like “72 pixels per inch (ppi)”, but for screen-based work this value is often misunderstood.

For digital displays:

resolution is determined by pixel dimensions (width × height), not pixels per inch

For example:

1920 × 1080 pixels looks the same image-wise

  • on a phone
  • on a monitor
  • on a cinema screen

Only the physical size changes, not the number of pixels.

docs.title: From Grayscale to Colour
docs.text:
In the previous chapter, we explored grayscale images.

Now we expand that idea:

  • each channel (R, G, B) is itself a grayscale image
  • each pixel stores a grayscale value per channel

For example (8-bit per channel):

  • Red = 255
  • Green = 0
  • Blue = 0

→ the result is pure red

Another example:

  • Red = 255
  • Green = 255
  • Blue = 255

→ the result is white

docs.title: Bit Depth in Colour Images
docs.text:
In most common RGB images:

  • each channel uses 8 bits
  • that gives 256 values per channel

Since there are 3 channels:

256 × 256 × 256 = 16.7 million possible colours

This is why 8-bit RGB images are often called “24-bit colour” (8 bits × 3 channels).

Higher bit depths (like 16-bit per channel) allow for:

  • smoother gradients
  • more accurate colour representation
  • better editing results

REDGREENBLUE

docs.title: Alpha Channel (Transparency)
docs.text:
In addition to Red, Green, and Blue, many images include a fourth channel:

  • Alpha

The alpha channel controls transparency:

  • 0 → fully transparent
  • 1 (or max value) → fully visible

This is essential in animation for:

  • layering images
  • compositing
  • visual effects

Transparency will be explored further in a later chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Most screens use the RGB colour model
  • Each pixel consists of three components: red, green, blue
  • RGB is an additive system (adding light creates white)
  • An RGB image consists of three grayscale channels
  • Combined channels produce full colour
  • Standard images use 8 bits per channel (24-bit colour total)
  • Resolution for screens is defined by pixel dimensions, not ppi

Coming Up Next: Chapter 6 – Colour Spaces and Colour Management

In the next chapter, we take a deeper look at how colour is organized, stored, and interpreted inside digital systems.

docs.title: What Is Transparency
docs.text:
So far, we have looked at how images are built using pixels, resolution, and colour. Each pixel stores colour information using RGB values.

However, there is another important aspect of an image:

whether a pixel is visible or not

It’s a channel just like RGB but it determines if whether a pixel is visible or not. Transparency determines:

  • which pixels of an image are visible
  • which pixels are hidden
  • and how transparent or opaque an pixel is

For example:

  • a character can be drawn without a background
  • allowing it to be placed on top of another image

Without transparency, every image would always be a solid rectangle.

docs.title: The Alpha Channel
docs.text:
Transparency is stored in an additional channel called the alpha channel.

Just like RGB:

  • red → one channel
  • green → one channel
  • blue → one channel

Alpha is:

  • one extra channel
  • storing visibility instead of colour

This channel is often called A. So we have RGBA if we need transparency.

How Alpha Works

The alpha channel is a greyscale image:

  • 0 → fully transparent (invisible)
  • 0.5 → semi-transparent
  • 1 → fully opaque (visible)

This is the same idea as bit depth and greyscale values discussed earlier.

The alpha channel controls how much of a pixel you can see.

RGBA Images

When RGB and Alpha are combined, we get:

RGBA = Red, Green, Blue, Alpha

This means each pixel contains:

  • colour information
  • visibility information

docs.title: Layers and Image Stacking
docs.text:
Transparency allows images to be placed on top of each other.

This is commonly referred to as layers.

  • background layer → sits at the bottom
  • foreground layer → sits on top
  • each layer can have transparency

How Layers Work

When stacking images:

  • visible pixels cover what is below
  • transparent pixels reveal what is underneath

This makes it possible to:

  • combine drawings
  • add effects
  • build complex images step by step

docs.title: Basic Compositing
docs.text:
The process of combining images using transparency is called:

compositing

In compositing:

  • multiple images are layered
  • the alpha channel determines how they interact

Simple Example

Imagine:

  • a character on a transparent background
  • placed on top of a landscape

The result:

  • the character appears inside the scene
  • without any visible rectangle around it

This is possible because:

the transparent pixels allow the background to show through

Why Compositing Matters

Compositing is used in:

  • animation
  • visual effects
  • motion graphics

It allows different elements to be:

  • created separately
  • and combined into one final image

docs.title: Common Problems
docs.text:
Working with transparency can introduce issues.

Edge Halos

Sometimes you may see:

  • light or dark edges around objects

This happens when:

  • background colour information is mixed into the edges

Premultiplied vs Straight Alpha

There are two ways alpha can be stored Straight (unassociated) alpha and Premultiplied alpha.

Straight Alpha:

  • RGB stores original colour values
  • alpha is applied separately

Premultiplied Alpha:

  • RGB values are already multiplied by alpha
  • useful for rendering and compositing

Why This Matters

If these types are mixed incorrectly:

  • edges may appear wrong
  • transparency may not behave as expected

docs.title: Practical Examples
docs.text:
In practice, transparency is used in many everyday situations.

Example 1 – Character Animation

  • characters are drawn without backgrounds
  • placed over scenes

Example 2 – Visual Effects

  • smoke, fire, or light effects
  • layered on top of footage

Example 3 – User Interface Design

  • icons with transparent backgrounds
  • used across different layouts

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Transparency controls whether a pixel is visible
  • The alpha channel stores transparency as greyscale
  • RGBA images combine colour and visibility
  • Layers allow images to be stacked
  • Compositing combines images into a final result
  • Incorrect handling can cause visual artefacts

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
In the previous chapter, we explored RGB colour and bit depth—how software represents and calculates colour.

However, there is another important layer to understand:

how colour is actually displayed on different devices

What you see on your screen is not only determined by software, but also by hardware, such as:

  • monitors
  • televisions
  • projectors
  • cinema screens

And here’s the challenge:

No two screens display colour exactly the same.

docs.title: The Problem: Devices Show Colour Differently
docs.text:
Different displays can vary in:

  • brightness
  • contrast
  • colour saturation
  • colour accuracy

Even two screens of the same model can look slightly different.

This means:

  • the colours you create on one device
  • may look different on another

For example:

  • an image that looks bright on your laptop
  • might appear darker on another screen or vice versa

docs.title: The Solution: Colour Spaces and ICC Profiles
docs.text:
To solve this problem, the industry introduced standardized systems for handling colour.

One of the most important concepts is Colour Spaces

A colour space defines a range of colours that can be represented.

You can think of it as: a shared “language” for colour

ICC Profiles

An ICC profile (International Colour Consortium profile) describes how a specific device handles colour.

It acts as a translator:

  • it converts colours from a device-specific format
  • into a common colour space
  • and back again for another device

So the workflow becomes:

Device A → ICC profile → common colour space → ICC profile → Device B

This ensures that colours remain as consistent as possible across different screens.

docs.title: Colour Gamut
docs.text:
Each device can only display a limited range of colours.

This range is called the:

colour gamut

  • some devices show a small range of colours
  • others can display wider, more saturated colours

docs.title: The CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram
docs.text:
To visualize colour gamut’s, we use a standard model:

CIE 1931 Colour Model

This model maps colours visible to the human eye.

  • it represents colours based on wavelengths (nanometers)
  • usually shown as a 2D diagram (chromaticity diagram)
  • although real colour perception is three-dimensional

This diagram is not perfect, but it provides a useful reference for comparing colour spaces.

docs.title: Common Colour Spaces
docs.text:
Different industries use different standard colour spaces.

sRGB

  • Most common colour space for:
  • screens
  • web content
  • Designed as a standard for consistency
  • Relatively limited colour range

Adobe RGB

  • Wider colour gamut than sRGB
  • Designed to include more colours used in printing (CMYK)
  • Better for professional image work

Even so:

it still covers only a portion of all visible colours

Beyond sRGB and Adobe RGB

As technology improves, newer displays support:

  • wide colour gamuts
  • more accurate colour reproduction

These are sometimes visualized using extended versions of colour models, such as:

  • CIExyY (which also includes brightness)

For now, understanding the basic CIE 1931 diagram is sufficient.

docs.title: Comparing Gamut’s
docs.text:
When different colour spaces are plotted on the same diagram:

  • sRGB covers a certain area
  • Adobe RGB covers a larger area
  • modern displays may go even beyond

This shows:

not every device can display every colour

docs.title: Why This Matters in Animation
docs.text:
When working in animation:

  • you might create an image in one colour space
  • view it on another device
  • export it to a different format

Without proper colour management:

  • colours can shift
  • images can look inconsistent

Understanding colour spaces helps you:

  • maintain consistent visuals
  • avoid unexpected colour changes
  • prepare work for different outputs (screen, web, print)

docs.title: A Note on Calibration
docs.text:
Even with colour spaces and ICC profiles, screens can still be inaccurate.

This is why professionals use:

calibration

Calibration adjusts a display so it matches a known standard.

This topic will be explored further in a later chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Different devices display colour differently
  • A colour space defines a shared range of colours
  • An ICC profile translates colour between devices
  • A colour gamut is the range of colours a device can display
  • The CIE 1931 model is used to visualize colour spaces
  • sRGB and Adobe RGB are common standards
  • No device can display the full range of visible colours

Coming Up Next

Chapter 10 – Colour Management in Practice

In the next chapter, we will look at how to work with colour spaces, ICC profiles, and calibration in a real animation workflow.

docs.title: Colour Management in Practice
docs.text:
Consistent Colour Starts with Your Screen

In the previous chapter, we introduced colour spaces as a way to standardise colour across different devices. A colour space defines which colours can be represented.

However, this system only works if your display actually shows those colours correctly.

A colour space is a standard—but your monitor is not.

Every display:

  • is manufactured differently
  • ages over time
  • has its own brightness, contrast, and colour response

Even two identical monitors can show slightly different colours.

The Problem Without Calibration

If your monitor is not calibrated, the colours you see are not reliable.

For example:

  • colours may appear too warm or too cool
  • shadows may look too dark or too bright
  • saturation may be exaggerated or muted

This leads to a critical issue:

You are making decisions based on incorrect visual information.

In practice, this means:

  • your animation looks different on another properly calibrated screen
  • colours shift when viewed on a phone, TV, or projector
  • collaboration becomes unreliable

Calibration as a Reference Point

Monitor calibration solves this problem by bringing your display to a known standard.

Calibration adjusts your monitor so that it matches common reference values such as:

  • White point (D65 / 6500K)
  • Gamma (usually 2.2)
  • Brightness (around 120 cd/m² for editing)

These values are not arbitrary—they are part of the same standardised system used in colour spaces.

Link to Colour Spaces (Key Concept)

Colour spaces (such as sRGB or DCI-P3) define:

which colours should be displayed

Calibration ensures:

your monitor displays those colours correctly

Without calibration:

  • the colour space is correct
  • but your display interpretation is wrong

With calibration:

  • your display becomes a reliable reference

The Role of ICC Profiles

After calibration, your system creates or applies an ICC profile.

An ICC profile describes:

  • how your specific monitor behaves
  • how it differs from an ideal standard

Software uses this profile to:

  • correct colour output
  • translate between devices

In this way, the ICC profile acts as a bridge between your monitor and the colour space.

Why This Matters in Practice

In a real production pipeline:

  • your work is viewed on multiple devices
  • each device interprets colour slightly differently

Calibration + ICC profiles help ensure that:

  • colours remain consistent
  • creative decisions remain valid
  • the final result matches your intention

Without calibration:

  • colour becomes unpredictable
  • workflows break down
  • results become unreliable

A Practical Perspective

For student-level work:

  • calibration may not always be perfect
  • default settings may still be used

However:

Even a basic calibration improves consistency significantly.

In professional environments:

  • calibration is standard practice
  • displays are regularly checked and recalibrated

Summary

  • Colour spaces define how colours should behave
  • Monitors interpret those colours differently
  • Calibration aligns your monitor with a known standard
  • ICC profiles translate between devices
  • Together, they make colour consistent and predictable
